text: The surgeon places classical laparoscopic ports for cholecystectomy: one in the epigastrium, one in the hypogastrium, and a third in the lumbar region, with the camera port positioned at the umbilicus.

Using the lumbar port, the surgeon retracts the gallbladder upward, revealing an abnormal bulge in the gallbladder area. The peritoneum over the swelling is dissected, exposing a cord-like structure running downward.

The surgeon removes the peritoneum above the gallbladder, revealing a slow outpouching identified as Hartmann's pouch. The cystic artery appears as a taut structure, abnormally positioned towards the right and anterior to the cystic duct.

The surgeon completes the dissection of Hartmann's pouch and proceeds to dissect the cystic artery. The long cystic duct is clearly visible, along with the junction between the cystic duct and common bile duct (CBD). The T junction is prominently displayed, reducing the risk of CBD injury during the laparoscopic cholecystectomy.

The surgeon identifies the cystic artery, which is abnormally positioned towards the right side of the cystic duct. A window is created between the cystic artery and the cystic duct, ensuring the artery leads into the body of the gallbladder. The surgeon then clips the cystic artery using LT clips.
The surgeon carefully clips the cystic artery, which is visibly entering the gallbladder, placing two clips on each side of the artery.

The surgeon divides the cystic artery, enhancing the mobility of the gallbladder. The previously abnormal-looking pouch is revealed to be part of the gallbladder, with the cystic artery having contributed to its unusual appearance.

The surgeon creates a classical posterior window and begins dissecting the cystic duct, dividing unnamed vessels around it. The infundibulum cystic duct junction is being identified, and the abnormal outpouching of Hartman's pouch is now visible, clarifying the anatomy.

The surgeon creates a wide posterior window, ensuring the critical view of safety, with no linear structures other than the cystic duct and an abnormal vessel going into the gallbladder. The cystic duct is skeletonized, revealing its parallel orientation to the abnormal vessel, in preparation for ligation and division.

The surgeon divides the cystic duct between clips, with a Tenomum clip applied above the cystic duct-common bile duct junction. The proximal side clips are visible, and additional clips are placed on the distal side at the cystic duct-infundibulum junction.

The surgeon places pairs of clips on the cystic duct before dividing it. The gallbladder is then removed from its bed by dividing the mesogallbladder or peritoneal fold connecting the gallbladder bed to the gallbladder.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to carefully dissect the mesogallbladder, maintaining a gentle pace similar to the dissection of Calot's triangle. The gallbladder is gradually detached from the liver bed, ensuring precision and patience throughout the procedure.

The surgeon carefully divides the peritoneal attachment of the gallbladder to the liver, progressing towards the fundus. The dissection is completed by reversing the approach to detach the gallbladder fully.

The surgeon observes the disappearance of the cystic dilatation, initially mistaken for Hartmann's pouch, after dividing the cystic artery. The posterior window is dissected to clearly separate the cystic duct and artery, ensuring no ductal abnormalities are present.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the gallbladder, detaching it from the liver bed. The absence of a cholecystohepatic duct, known as the duct of Lushka, is confirmed, marking the completion of the cholecystectomy procedure.

The surgeon uses a clofosub instrument to grasp the gallbladder at the cystic duct end and carefully extracts it through the epigastric tenenum port. The gallbladder is gently manipulated and removed from the surgical field.
